planting.note: Direct sow in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral woodland soil in partial shade for natural winter stratification and spring germination; beaked agrimony thrives in the dappled understory of deciduous forests and is an excellent low-care native for shaded naturalistic plantings.

directions: Direct sow in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral forest soil in partial shade; cold stratification over winter improves germination in spring. Agrimonia rostellata (beaked agrimony) is a native perennial of eastern North American deciduous forests, typically found in moist, shaded ravines, hillside woodlands, and forest margins, where it produces erect, slender stems with pinnately compound, coarsely toothed leaves and slender, wand-like racemes of small, bright yellow, 5-petaled flowers from late summer into fall — providing one of the few reliable yellow-flowering options for shaded native gardens in the late season. The hooked seed capsules that follow bloom cling readily to fur and clothing, dispersing by animals in the classic agrimony fashion. This is a quieter, more refined plant than the showier prairie wildflowers, suited to a naturalistic woodland where its delicate flowers and functional seed dispersal biology will be appreciated. Good companion for asters, goldenrods, and wild gingers at woodland edges.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; soft agrimony is distinguished from other agrimonies by its softly hairy stems and leaves and is native to rich, moist deciduous forests throughout eastern North America.

directions: Direct sow in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral woodland soil in partial to full shade for spring germination after natural cold stratification. Soft agrimony is distinguished from the related beaked agrimony (A. rostellata) by its taller stature, softly hairy stems and foliage, and larger leaflets — the downy texture gives the whole plant a gentle, velvety appearance. Like other agrimonies, it produces slender, vertical spikes of small, bright yellow, 5-petaled flowers from mid-summer into fall that provide modest late-season color in shaded native plantings, followed by hooked seed capsules that disperse by attaching to animal fur. Soft agrimony grows in rich, moist, slightly acidic to neutral deciduous forest soils throughout eastern North America and is a good, low-maintenance filler plant for the shaded native garden, especially effective when naturalized in large masses under tall forest canopy.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; Virginia mountain mint is the most heat-tolerant mountain mint species, thriving in zone 8–9 where its northern relatives decline, and is an exceptional pollinator plant for the Gulf South.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in well-drained to moderately moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; Virginia mountain mint is the most heat-tolerant and southeastern species of the mountain mints, thriving through the summer of zone 8–9 where its more northerly relatives like P. virginianum begin to decline. Pycnanthemum pycnanthemoides produces erect, branching stems with lance-shaped, strongly mint-scented leaves and the flat-topped clusters of tiny, white, purple-dotted flowers that are the genus hallmark, blooming July through September and attracting an astonishing diversity of native bees, wasps, beetles, butterflies, and skippers. Mountain mints are among the most comprehensively documented native pollinator plants in eastern North America, with studies recording over 100 insect species on a single planting. The plant spreads by rhizomes into a colony but is not aggressive; divide every 3–4 years to maintain vigor and share with other gardeners. Excellent in naturalistic meadow plantings, rain gardens, and native border plantings from Virginia through the Gulf Coast.

planting.note: Plant bulb divisions in spring in moist to wet, strongly acidic, sandy or peaty soil in partial shade to full sun; osceola plume requires consistently moist, acidic, low-nutrient conditions similar to those of Gulf Coast pine flatwoods and pitcher plant bogs.

directions: Plant bulb divisions in spring in moist to wet, strongly acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), sandy or peaty soil in partial shade to full sun; osceola plume (formerly Zigadenus densus) is a native bulbous perennial of Gulf Coast and South Atlantic pine flatwoods, wet savannas, and pitcher plant bogs from Florida to North Carolina, producing narrow, grass-like basal leaves and slender flowering stems 2–4 feet tall bearing a dense, showy, plume-like raceme of small white flowers in May and June — one of the most floriferous and ornamental members of the bunchflower family. It is an excellent companion plant in acidic bog gardens alongside pitcher plants (Sarracenia), sundews (Drosera), rose pogonia orchids, and grass-pink orchids. All parts of the plant are toxic if ingested; do not confuse with edible bulbs. The species has a limited native range in the coastal plain and is becoming commercially available through specialty native plant nurseries as interest in Gulf Coast bog gardens grows.

planting.note: Plant rhizome/corm divisions in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic woodland or meadow soil in partial shade to full sun; Appalachian bunchflower grows naturally in rich mountain woodlands and moist mountain meadows and tolerates more shade than M. virginicum.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; Appalachian bunchflower (Melanthium hybridum) is closely related to Virginia bunchflower (M. virginicum) but differs in growing primarily in rich, moist Appalachian mountain woodlands and forest margins rather than coastal plain wet meadows, and in bearing leaves that are somewhat wider and more variable — the name 'hybridum' reflects the morphological variability of the species rather than hybrid origin. It produces the same stately, grass-clump form and tall summer flowering stems 3–5 feet tall topped with large panicles of cream-to-greenish-white flowers that age purple, providing a dramatic vertical accent in the shaded native garden. Less commercially available than M. virginicum but worth seeking from Appalachian native plant nurseries for its superior shade tolerance and ornamental appeal in forested garden settings. All parts are toxic; handle with gloves.
